explanation.text: After Aunt Marjorie leaves, the children know Mother's thrift and fashion sense are in conflict over the hat. They “held their breath” because they anxiously awaited her decision.
Paragraph 5 explains the story's central problem: Mother is too thrifty to discard the nearly new hat but cannot stand wearing it. This conflict motivates her later attempts to reuse it.
Mother's belief in using things up guides every attempt to find a purpose for the unwanted hat, including the successful scarecrow. The quotation therefore supports a theme of resourceful reuse.

The narrator exaggerates Pete dropping “like a stone,” suggests mouth-to-beak resuscitation, and compares the glowing flowers to glaring eyes. These playful details show that she views the mishap as funny.
Josh has just declared that the hat is not for the birds while Mother stands with one foot raised. The pause before she laughs makes the moment feel frozen because nobody moves or speaks.

The hat keeps birds away from the cornfield, but Great-aunt Marjorie, who gave Mother the hat, visits every June. Crows appearing only then implies Mother removes it so Marjorie will not see its use and feel hurt.
Both children wait to see what Mother will do, search for the missing hat, and watch as she considers destroying it. These shared actions show their interest in how she will solve her problem.

Oxen, sleds, and lantern light are presented as ordinary farm tools in Bentley's childhood. Those historical details therefore signal that the events occurred long before modern transportation and electric lighting.
The children are said to have “pelted snowballs at roosting crows.” Since snowballs are objects they would toss at the birds, “pelted” means threw.
Bentley works for years to preserve the beauty he sees in snowflakes so other people can see it too. His statement that “now everyone can see” directly expresses that central purpose.

Paragraph 9 describes Bentley waiting for hours in the cold, handling fragile crystals without breathing or twitching, and trying again after failures. This care shows the author's admiration for his dedication.

Bentley's parents spend savings equal to the value of ten cows on his camera, and later he spends every penny on his pictures. Together, these details show that his work required costly equipment he willingly funded.
Bentley gives photographs away, sells them for only a few cents, holds slide shows, and shares his findings in articles and speeches. These choices show he values sharing the work more than profit.
The account moves from Bentley's childhood observations to his teenage experiments and then to his recognition as a snow expert. Therefore, the progression organizes his life and study in time order.

Paragraph 1 introduces Gregor as a poor tenant farmer unable to afford the flock he wants. By paragraph 23 he owns a huge flock and shares a fortune, so the two paragraphs frame the change in his life.
MacTavish has begun to leave, but he turns and “marches back” when Gregor refuses to sell, then offers to pay anything. His forceful return shows his determination to obtain the sheep.
The seller persuades Gregor that a sickly sheep can create wealth, while Gregor spreads the same claim to lure MacTavish. Both men therefore use a trick about the sheep to make money.

After becoming rich, Gregor shares his remaining fortune with families MacTavish had cheated. Therefore, his generosity distinguishes him from MacTavish, who withheld earnings and kept wealth for himself.
Gregor uses his new wealth to help his mother and the poor families of the valley. MacTavish, in contrast, cheats those families and hoards houses and gems, showing that Gregor is the generous one.

MacTavish's desire to own anything makes him trade his mansion and all its contents for a supposedly magical sheep. Because his greed costs him that fortune, the story shows that greed can bring a great loss.
Because hearing rumors about a wealth-producing sheep draws MacTavish to Gregor and begins the bargain that transforms Gregor's life, that turning point belongs in a summary more than minor details.

The mugumo provides food and a home for animals, holds the spirits of Kikuyu ancestors, and inspires Wangari's promise to protect it. These details therefore develop its importance to both people and animals.
A seed in rich soil is prepared to keep growing, just as Wangari's mind is ready to develop through more education. The simile therefore shows that she wants to continue learning.

Paragraph 13 describes companies cutting forests, and paragraph 14 traces the results: lost habitats, eroding soil, muddy rivers, and hunger. The details therefore move from an environmental cause to its effects.
Wangari carries seedlings and tools to villages, towns, and cities and urges mothers to plant with her. Therefore, traveling to women across Kenya is how she shares and spreads the idea nationwide.

Wangari calls on women to work together, travels throughout Kenya, and helps the Green Belt Movement plant thirty million trees. That sustained group effort shows her belief in cooperation and determination.

The “seeds of change” are the trees Wangari persuades people to plant. Those plantings restore forests, clean water, food, and habitats, so the title points to how she solves the environmental problem.

As a girl, Wangari's mother teaches her that the mugumo shelters and feeds many lives and must not be cut down. That early lesson connects directly to Wangari's later plan to restore Kenya by planting trees.
